one_line_takeaway: MEF is a principled, low-cost transfer-based black-box attack that unifies fragmented 'flatness' heuristics under a first theoretical ATG bound and, by optimizing zeroth-order worst-neighborhood average flatness with gradient-reusing NCS/GBO, beats the prior SOTA (PGN) by 4-8% ASR at half to equal compute across 33 models, defenses, commercial APIs, and MLLMs.
key_finding: Optimizing a maximin zeroth-order average-case flatness metric yields state-of-the-art transferability at lower compute. On normally-trained models MEF_H reaches 89.93% average ASR at HALF the backprop cost of the prior SOTA PGN (88.20%), and MEF_F reaches 94.84% at matching cost. Gains hold across ViT/MLP architectures, model ensembles, seven defenses (MEF_F 64.30% avg, +8.46 over PGN; nearly doubling PGN on the robust MeanSparse model, 21.1% vs 11.6%), six commercial APIs (Google Cloud Vision T-1 79.40% vs PGN 67.20%), and MLLMs (36.5% avg fooling rate). Efficiency: MEF_H uses 200 backprops / 0.46s / 4090 MiB vs PGN's 400 / 1.13s / 4488 MiB, a 2.4x speedup while +4.3% ASR. MEF also resists surrogate overfitting, climbing to a peak 96.40% ASR at T=36 while PGN plateaus.
core_contribution: The paper proposes MEF (Maximin Expected Flatness), a transfer-based black-box adversarial attack, together with (a) the first unifying taxonomy of 'flatness-enhanced' transfer attacks under a k-th-order worst-case vs average-case flatness framework, and (b) the first theoretical link between loss-landscape flatness and adversarial transferability. The paper's own analysis (Sec. III-B, Fig. 2) shows that prior worst-case methods (RAP, PGN) over-explore sensitivity peaks (low inter-update gradient similarity, 0.10-0.25) while average-case methods (FEM, TPA) over-exploit plateaus (high similarity, 0.38-0.88). To resolve this, the authors define a new 'zeroth-order worst-neighborhood average flatness' metric R-hat^(0)_{gamma,xi} and optimize it as a maximin objective, realized with two efficiency mechanisms the paper introduces: Neighborhood Conditional Sampling (NCS, gradient-guided worst-region search) and Gradient Balancing Optimization (GBO, which reuses outer-maximization gradients for the inner minimization to cut backprops ~50%). NOTE: the taxonomy in Table I catalogs cited prior methods (RAP/GNP/PGN/FEM/APP/TPA); the contribution is unifying them, not those methods themselves. MEF, NCS, GBO, the ATG definition, and Theorem 1 are the paper's own work.
threat_model: Transfer-based, fully query-free black-box evasion attack. The attacker has white-box access to a surrogate classifier (e.g., ResNet-50) but only black-box, zero-query access to the victim. Adversarial examples are crafted on the surrogate under an L-infinity budget (default epsilon=16/255) and deployed directly against unknown targets, avoiding both the unrealistic model-access assumption of white-box attacks and the prohibitive query cost / detectability of query-based attacks. Targets include normally trained CNNs, cross-architecture models (ViT/MLP-Mixer), adversarially-trained and purification/certified defenses, six commercial vision APIs, and multimodal LLMs. The paper's ATG (Adversarial Transferability Gap) formalization assumes surrogate and target share a label space, use the same loss, and are aligned on clean inputs.
limitations: The paper's own Discussion concedes: (1) an inherent transferability-vs-stealthiness trade-off in the zero-query setting, since transfer attacks need a relatively large perturbation budget (epsilon=16/255 default) to generalize; (2) strictly bridging the structural gap between surrogate and target remains open. Additional constraints from my reading: Theorem 1 rests on strong alignment assumptions (shared label space, identical loss, matched clean accuracy) and local Taylor approximability, and the zeroth-order dominance argument is justified via the N~1 ReLU regime. Absolute ASR against strong adaptive/RobustBench defenses stays low (e.g. 15.8-21.1% on MeanSparse), so 'defeats defenses' is relative to baselines, not absolute. As a purely offensive contribution, no new defense is proposed. Several full comparisons and all theorem proofs are deferred to supplementary material not evaluated here.

datasets_used: ImageNet-compatible dataset (1,000 images, 299x299 with annotated labels)
100-image ImageNet subset (used for optimization-dynamics, GBO, and efficiency micro-studies)
quantitative_results: Normally-trained models (Table II): MEF_H 89.93% avg ASR at half PGN's cost vs PGN 88.20%; MEF_F 94.84% at matching cost; per-surrogate MEF_F AVG up to 96.3 (Res-50) and 96.7 (Res-101)
Defenses (Table X, 7 protected targets): MEF_H 60.70% avg (+4.86 over PGN 55.84%); MEF_F 64.30% (+8.46)
Advanced defenses (Table XI): MEF_F 21.1% on MeanSparse vs PGN 11.6% (Res-50 surrogate); DiffPure 48.6% vs PGN 46.9%
Commercial APIs (Table VIII): MEF_F Google Cloud Vision 79.40% T-1 vs PGN 67.20%; Alibaba Cloud 91.70% T-1
MLLMs (Table IX): MEF_F 36.5% avg CLIP fooling rate (Qwen2.5-VL 41.5%); MEF_H 31.5% avg vs best baseline FEM 26.9%
Efficiency (Table XIV): MEF_H 200 backprops / 0.46 s per image / 4090 MiB / 90.6% ASR vs PGN 400 BP / 1.13 s / 4488 MiB / 86.3% ASR (2.4x speedup, +4.3% ASR)
Convergence (Table XII): MEF peak ASR 96.40% at T=36 (+6.6% over T=10) vs PGN 88.50% peak
Low-budget robustness: at epsilon=4/255 MEF 41.3% ASR vs PGN 31.7% (Res-50 -> Inc-v3)
Perceptual quality (Table XIII): MEF_H SSIM 0.721 / PSNR 28.35 / LPIPS 0.250, best fidelity among compared attacks; MEF_F highest ASR 95.1%
Gradient-similarity study (Fig. 2): worst-case mean sim RAP 0.10, PGN 0.25 vs average-case FEM 0.38, TPA 0.88
Flatness metrics (Table XV, xi=32/255): MEF_H lowest zeroth-order flatness (Res-50 2.20, Inc-v3 1.09); MEF_F lowest first-order (0.10 / 0.21)
Scale of study: 33 models and 43 transfer-based attacks compared
baselines_compared: Gradient-stabilization: EMI, ANDA, FGSRA, GI, MUMODIG, GAA, FoolMix, MI, NI, PI, VMI, VNI
Input-augmentation: TI, DI, SI, Admix, SSA, STM, USMM, DeCoWA, L2T, BSR, OPS
Model-driven / feature / generative: SGM, LinBP, AWT, FAUG, ANA, LL2S, ILA, NAA, BFA, P2FA, CDA, DiffAttack, SVRE, CWA
